title: Congee with Century Egg

seo.title: Authentic Century Egg Congee Recipe | Easy Chinese Breakfast

seo.imageAlt: A bowl of creamy authentic Century Egg Congee garnished with green onions and a slice of century egg.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a comforting bowl of homemade Century Egg Congee, a classic Chinese breakfast.

description: Congee with Century Egg is a traditional Chinese breakfast, a warm and comforting rice porridge known for its unique savory flavor. The creamy texture of the congee perfectly complements the distinct, earthy taste of the century egg, creating a deeply satisfying meal. Often garnished with fresh ginger and green onions, it is a staple in many Chinese households for its nourishing qualities.

ingredients.notes: peeled and thinly sliced or julienned

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse the white rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. For a creamier congee, you can soak the rice in water for at least 30 minutes, or even overnight, before cooking. Drain well.

instructions.title: Start the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large pot, combine the rinsed rice with 8-10 cups of water or chicken broth. The amount of liquid depends on your desired consistency; start with 8 cups and add more if needed. Bring the mixture to a boil over high heat.

instructions.title: Simmer the Congee
instructions.text: Once boiling, reduce the heat to low, cover the pot partially, and let it simmer for at least 30-45 minutes. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking and ensure even cooking. The rice grains should break down and create a thick, creamy porridge.

instructions.title: Prepare Century Eggs and Ginger
instructions.text: While the congee simmers, peel the century eggs. Rinse them briefly and then dice them into small, bite-sized pieces. Prepare the fresh ginger by peeling and thinly slicing or julienning it. Set aside some ginger and green onions for garnish.

instructions.title: Add Century Eggs and Season
instructions.text: Once the congee has reached your desired creamy consistency, add the diced century eggs and most of the prepared ginger into the pot. Stir well to combine. Season the congee with salt and white pepper to taste. Continue to simmer for another 5-10 minutes to allow the flavors to meld.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Ladle the hot Century Egg Congee into individual serving bowls. Drizzle a small amount of sesame oil over each bowl. Garnish generously with the chopped green onions and the reserved fresh ginger slices or juliennes. Serve immediately and enjoy this comforting Chinese breakfast.

tips: For extra creamy congee, freeze the washed rice overnight before cooking. The freezing process breaks down the rice grains, allowing them to soften and disintegrate faster.
Do not overcook the century eggs in the congee; adding them towards the end helps preserve their unique texture and flavor without making them too rubbery.
Adjust the amount of water or broth to achieve your preferred congee consistency. For a thicker porridge, use less liquid; for a thinner one, add more hot water as it simmers.
article:
A steaming bowl of congee is the ultimate comfort food, a warm embrace that has nourished generations across Asia. While there are countless variations, one particular rendition stands out for its unique flavor profile and intriguing ingredients: Century Egg Congee. This quintessential Chinese breakfast recipe transforms simple rice porridge into a gourmet experience, offering a delightful blend of textures and an unforgettable savory depth. If you're looking to explore the rich tapestry of Chinese cuisine, learning to make this classic dish is an excellent place to start.

## What is Century Egg Congee?

At its heart, congee is a rice porridge, typically made by simmering rice in a large amount of water or broth until the grains break down into a creamy, soupy consistency. It's a versatile canvas, often served plain or enhanced with various toppings and ingredients. Century Egg Congee, known in Cantonese as *pidan shourou zhou* (preserved egg and lean pork congee), takes this humble base to another level with the addition of two key components: century eggs and often, lean shredded pork.

The star of the show, the century egg (also known as preserved egg or 100-year egg), is a Chinese delicacy. Despite its name, it’s not aged for a century, but typically for several weeks or months in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls. This curing process transforms a duck, chicken, or quail egg into a unique ingredient with a dark, gelatinous "white" (albumen) and a creamy, greenish-black yolk. The flavor is complex – earthy, savory, slightly pungent, with an umami richness that perfectly complements the mildness of the congee. When combined with tender shreds of pork and fragrant ginger, the Century Egg Congee becomes a symphony of taste and texture, a truly distinctive and satisfying breakfast.

## Ingredients for Perfect Century Egg Congee

Creating a truly delicious Century Egg Congee starts with selecting the right ingredients. Here’s what you’ll need:

*   **Rice:** 1 cup long-grain white rice (Jasmine rice works wonderfully for its fragrance and texture).
*   **Water or Broth:** 8-10 cups water or chicken/pork broth (broth adds significantly more flavor).
*   **Century Eggs:** 2-3 century eggs, peeled and diced into small pieces.
*   **Lean Pork:** 4-6 oz lean pork loin or tenderloin, thinly sliced or shredded (optional, but highly recommended).
*   **Ginger:** 1-inch piece of fresh ginger, peeled and julienned or finely minced.
*   **Scallions:** 2-3 scallions (green onions), thinly sliced for garnish.
*   **Sesame Oil:** 1 teaspoon for drizzling (optional, but adds aroma).
*   **Salt:** To taste.
*   **White Pepper:** To taste.
*   **Marinade for Pork (if using):** 1 tsp soy sauce, 1/2 tsp cornstarch, 1/2 tsp sesame oil, pinch of white pepper.

## Step-by-Step: How to Make Century Egg Congee

Making a creamy, flavorful Century Egg Congee is a patient process, but the results are incredibly rewarding.

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Rinse the rice thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. For an even creamier congee that cooks faster, you can soak the rice in 2 cups of water with 1 tablespoon of oil for at least 30 minutes, or even overnight. This helps the grains break down more easily.
2.  **Start the Congee Base:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot, combine the rinsed rice and 8-10 cups of water or broth. Bring it to a rolling boil over high heat.
3.  **Simmer and Stir:** Once boiling, reduce the heat to low, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap for steam to escape), and let it simmer. Stir occasionally, especially at the bottom, to prevent the rice from sticking. This slow simmering process should take 1 to 1.5 hours, or until the rice grains have burst open and the congee has thickened to a creamy, uniform consistency. If it becomes too thick, add more hot water or broth.
4.  **Prepare the Pork (if using):** While the congee simmers, marinate the shredded pork with soy sauce, cornstarch, sesame oil, and white pepper for at least 15 minutes.
5.  **Add Flavorings:** About 15-20 minutes before the congee is ready, add the julienned ginger and the marinated pork (if using) to the pot. Stir well to separate the pork shreds and ensure they cook through.
6.  **Introduce the Century Eggs:** Once the pork is cooked and the congee has reached your desired consistency, gently fold in the diced century eggs. Simmer for another 5 minutes to allow the flavors to meld.
7.  **Season and Serve:** Season the congee with salt and white pepper to taste. Ladle the hot Century Egg Congee into bowls, drizzle with a touch of sesame oil, and garnish generously with sliced scallions.

## Pro Tips for the Best Congee

Achieving that perfect, silky smooth congee texture and balanced flavor requires a few insider tricks:

*   **Use Broth:** While water works, chicken or pork broth will significantly deepen the flavor profile of your congee, making it much richer and more savory.
*   **Don't Skimp on Soaking:** Soaking the rice, especially with a little oil, helps the grains swell and break down faster and more evenly, leading to a creamier texture.
*   **Low and Slow is Key:** Congee is not a dish to rush. A long, gentle simmer allows the rice to fully disintegrate and release its starches, creating that signature velvety consistency.
*   **Stir Regularly:** Especially in the beginning and towards the end of cooking, stir the congee frequently to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom of the pot and burning.
*   **Adjust Consistency:** Don't be afraid to add more hot water or broth as the congee cooks if it becomes too thick. It should be thick but pourable.
*   **Fresh Ginger is a Must:** Ginger not only adds a wonderful aroma but also helps to balance the unique, pungent notes of the century egg.
*   **Season at the End:** Add salt gradually and taste as you go. The saltiness of the century egg and broth will contribute to the overall flavor, so adjust accordingly.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Century Egg Congee is a complete meal in itself, but it truly shines when served hot and fresh with a few traditional accompaniments.

*   **Garnishes Galore:** Beyond scallions and sesame oil, consider a dash of white pepper, a sprinkle of crispy fried shallots, or a drizzle of chili oil for those who like a kick.
*   **Youtiao (Chinese Doughnuts):** The classic pairing! These long, deep-fried dough sticks are perfect for dipping into the hot congee, soaking up all its comforting flavors.
*   **Pickled Vegetables:** A small side of pickled radish or mustard greens offers a refreshing, tangy counterpoint to the rich congee.
*   **Soy Sauce:** A splash of light soy sauce can enhance the umami if desired.
*   **Other Dim Sum:** If serving for a larger breakfast spread, Century Egg Congee pairs wonderfully with other dim sum items like steamed buns or dumplings.

## Variations and Add-ins

While Century Egg Congee is delicious in its traditional form, there are many ways to customize it to your liking:

*   **Salted Duck Egg:** For an even richer, more complex flavor, add a diced salted duck egg along with the century egg. The creamy, salty yolk is a fantastic complement.
*   **Shredded Chicken:** If you prefer chicken over pork, simply use shredded cooked chicken breast or thigh.
*   **Seafood:** For a luxurious touch, add cooked shrimp, flaked fish, or scallops towards the end of the cooking process.
*   **Mushrooms:** Sliced shiitake mushrooms add an earthy depth.
*   **Peanuts:** Roasted peanuts sprinkled on top offer a nice crunch.
*   **Vegetarian Version:** Omit the pork and use vegetable broth. While century eggs are traditionally not vegetarian due to some curing agents, some brands may offer vegetarian-friendly versions.

## Frequently Asked Questions

**Q: What does century egg taste like?**
A: Century eggs have a unique, savory, and earthy flavor. The white is gelatinous and mild, while the yolk is creamy, intensely savory, and slightly pungent with an umami depth. It's an acquired taste for some, but many find it incredibly delicious, especially when balanced in dishes like Century Egg Congee.

**Q: Is century egg safe to eat?**
A: Yes, absolutely! Century eggs are a preserved food and are perfectly safe to eat. The preservation process changes their appearance and texture, but they are not raw or spoiled.

**Q: Can I make congee ahead of time?**
A: Yes, congee can be made ahead of time. It will thicken considerably as it cools. When reheating, add a bit of water or broth to reach your desired consistency and gently warm it over low heat, stirring frequently. Add fresh garnishes before serving.

**Q: Can I use brown rice for congee?**
A: While white rice is traditional, you can use brown rice. Keep in mind that brown rice will require significantly more liquid and a longer cooking time (often 2-3 hours) to break down into a creamy congee texture.

Century Egg Congee is more than just a breakfast dish; it's a culinary journey into the heart of Chinese comfort food. Its unique flavors, comforting warmth, and nourishing qualities make it a perfect start to any day. Don't be intimidated by the century egg; embrace its distinct character, and you'll discover a truly rewarding and delicious experience that you'll want to revisit again and again.
